text.title: Prompt Engineering Fundamentals — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the prompt-engineering-fundamentals methodology. Sources: Anthropic prompt-engineering documentation; OpenAI prompt-engineering guide; Google Gemini prompt design strategies.

rule.statement: Structure prompts in six blocks: system, context, task, examples, output format, constraints; never collapse them into a single wall of prose.
rule.rationale: Blocked prompts are parseable, diff-able, and editable in isolation; wall-of-prose prompts are unmaintainable past three iterations.

rule.statement: State the exact output format (JSON Schema reference, regex, or worked example) in the prompt; never leave format to model inference.
rule.rationale: Implicit format is the dominant failure mode in production; explicit format collapses tail risk.

rule.statement: Use 2–5 few-shot examples whenever the task format is novel or the model has never seen the output shape in training.
rule.rationale: Few-shot pins the output shape; zero-shot relies on the model's prior, which drifts between model versions.

rule.statement: Use the exact chat template the model was trained with (Claude conversational, Llama-3 instruct, OpenAI system/user); never paste cross-family prompts.

rationale.source: Brown et al. — Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (arXiv:2005.14165)

rule.rationale: Cross-family prompts produce silently degraded outputs; a Llama-3 template against GPT-4 may work but is fragile under retries.

rule.statement: Add every failed eval case to the test suite before patching the prompt; iterate on prompt + suite together.
rule.rationale: Patching the prompt without growing the suite makes the suite blind to the bug class that caused the failure.
